learning_objectives: Design fluid layouts that adapt gracefully across viewport sizes
Apply responsive design breakpoints strategically based on content needs
Create component-level responsive behavior rather than page-level breakpoints
readings: Responsive Web Design by Ethan Marcotte, Ch. 1-3
Inclusive Design Patterns by Heydon Pickering, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: The web is inherently fluid. Responsive design is not about making a desktop site fit on mobile — it is about designing systems that work at any viewport size, from smartwatch to ultrawide monitor.
key_concepts: Fluid grids: percentage-based columns that scale with viewport
Content-driven breakpoints vs. device-driven breakpoints
Container queries: component-level responsive behavior
Responsive typography: fluid type scales using clamp()
Art direction: different image crops and compositions for different sizes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a responsive card grid in Figma using auto-layout and constraints, then testing it across mobile, tablet, and desktop frames

review.value: Instead of breaking at 768px because that is an iPad width, break when the content breaks — when a line of text becomes too long, when a grid becomes too cramped. Content dictates breakpoints, not devices.
Responsive behavior based on the parent container size rather than the viewport — a card component adapts its layout based on how much space it has, working correctly in any context

learning_objectives: Design web navigation systems for sites ranging from 5 to 5000 pages
Implement common page patterns: landing, listing, detail, dashboard, settings
Apply F-pattern and Z-pattern layout principles to web pages
readings: Don't Make Me Think by Steve Krug, Ch. 6-7
Designing Web Navigation by James Kalbach, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Web navigation must handle a wider range of content scales than mobile. From a 5-page portfolio to a 5000-page e-commerce catalog, the navigation system must help users find what they need efficiently.
key_concepts: Header navigation patterns: horizontal nav, mega menus, utility nav
Page patterns: landing (convert), listing (browse), detail (inform), dashboard (monitor)
Breadcrumb navigation for deep hierarchies
Footer as navigation: sitemap, secondary links, legal
Reading patterns: F-pattern for text-heavy pages, Z-pattern for marketing pages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a complete navigation system for a 200-page SaaS marketing site — header, footer, sidebar, and contextual navigation across different page types
setup_requirements: Site map from the demo project

review.value: A large dropdown panel showing multiple navigation columns organized by category — best for sites with many sections where users need to scan options quickly without multiple clicks
Pages that display collections of items (products, articles, search results) — must include filtering, sorting, pagination/infinite scroll, and clear item summaries

learning_objectives: Design web forms that maximize completion rates
Apply conversion optimization techniques to landing pages and checkout flows
Reduce form abandonment through progressive disclosure and smart defaults
readings: Web Form Design by Luke Wroblewski, Ch. 5-8
Landing Page Optimization by Tim Ash, Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Web forms are conversion bottlenecks. Every field you add, every confusing label, every validation error increases abandonment. Form design is business-critical UX work.
key_concepts: Form reduction: removing unnecessary fields increases completion rates
Multi-step forms: breaking long forms into manageable chunks with progress indication
Address autocomplete and payment autofill integration
Trust signals: security badges, testimonials, and privacy reassurance near submit buttons
Error recovery: inline validation, clear error messages, preserving user input
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a 15-field registration form into a 3-step progressive form with address autocomplete and inline validation

review.value: Expedia removed one field (Company Name) from their booking form and increased revenue by $12 million per year. Every unnecessary field costs conversions.
Visual cues that reduce anxiety near conversion points — security lock icons near payment forms, privacy policy links near email fields, money-back guarantees near purchase buttons

learning_objectives: Apply WCAG 2.1 AA guidelines to web designs
Design for keyboard navigation and screen reader compatibility
Test web designs for accessibility using automated and manual methods
readings: WCAG 2.1 Guidelines: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust
Inclusive Design Patterns by Heydon Pickering, Ch. 3-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Accessibility is not a feature — it is a legal requirement and ethical imperative. Approximately 15% of the world's population has some form of disability. Designing for accessibility improves the experience for everyone.
key_concepts: WCAG POUR principles: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust
Keyboard navigation: focus order, focus indicators, skip links
Screen reader design: semantic HTML, ARIA labels, heading hierarchy
Color and contrast: WCAG contrast ratios, color-independent meaning
Cognitive accessibility: plain language, consistent layout, clear wayfinding
lecture_notes.demonstration: Accessibility audit of a real website using axe DevTools, keyboard-only navigation, and VoiceOver screen reader — identifying and fixing issues live
setup_requirements: Screen reader (VoiceOver on Mac, NVDA on Windows)

review.value: Perceivable (content available to all senses), Operable (all functionality via keyboard), Understandable (content and controls are clear), Robust (works across assistive technologies)
The sequence in which interactive elements receive keyboard focus — must follow a logical reading order. CSS visual positioning does not change focus order; DOM order does.

class_schedule.topic: Web Performance and Core Web Vitals
learning_objectives: Design for web performance by understanding how design decisions impact load time
Optimize for Core Web Vitals: LCP, FID, CLS
Make design decisions that balance visual richness with performance
readings: Designing for Performance by Lara Hogan, Ch. 4-6

lecture_notes.introduction: Every 100ms of added load time costs conversions. UX designers must understand how their design decisions affect performance — image sizes, font loading, layout shifts, and JavaScript-dependent interactions.
key_concepts: Core Web Vitals: LCP (loading), FID/INP (interactivity), CLS (visual stability)
Layout shift prevention: reserving space for images, ads, and dynamic content
Font loading strategies: font-display swap, system font stacks, variable fonts
Image optimization: responsive srcset, WebP/AVIF formats, lazy loading
Critical rendering path: what must load before the page is usable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a landing page using Chrome Lighthouse — identifying CLS issues from unspecified image dimensions, LCP bottlenecks from hero images, and proposing design-level fixes

review.value: Measures visual stability — how much content shifts after initial render. Caused by images without dimensions, dynamic ad injection, and late-loading fonts. Designers prevent CLS by specifying aspect ratios and reserving space.

Time until the largest visible content element (usually hero image or heading) renders. Target: under 2.5 seconds. Designers affect LCP through image size, format choice, and above-the-fold content prioritization.

questions.question: A client wants their desktop design (3-column layout with large hero image) to 'just shrink' for mobile. Explain why this approach fails and describe how you would adapt the design responsively.
questions.answer: Simply shrinking a desktop layout fails because: (1) 3 columns at mobile width makes each column unreadably narrow. (2) A large hero image takes too long to load on mobile networks and dominates the small screen. (3) Desktop hover interactions do not work on touch devices. (4) Desktop navigation patterns (horizontal mega menus) do not fit mobile. Responsive adaptation: Stack the 3 columns into a single column on mobile, prioritizing the most important content first. Replace the large hero image with a cropped, optimized mobile version. Convert horizontal navigation into a bottom tab bar or hamburger menu. Replace hover interactions with tap interactions. The content priority may differ from desktop — mobile users in a different context may need different information first.

rubric.key: Explains why shrinking fails with specific reasons

questions.question: What is the difference between device-driven breakpoints and content-driven breakpoints? Which approach leads to better responsive designs and why?
questions.answer: Device-driven breakpoints are set at specific device widths (375px for iPhone, 768px for iPad, 1024px for laptop). Content-driven breakpoints are set wherever the content stops working well — when text lines become too long, when images become too small, or when a layout becomes cramped or awkwardly spaced. Content-driven breakpoints are superior because: (1) There are thousands of device sizes; you cannot target them all. (2) Content-driven breakpoints naturally adapt to any device, including future devices. (3) They focus on the actual problem (content readability) rather than an arbitrary target (device width). Best practice: start with the content at a narrow width and expand until the layout breaks, then add a breakpoint there.

rubric.key: Explains why content-driven is superior with reasoning

questions.question: A web page uses a custom dropdown menu built with div elements styled to look like a native select. List three accessibility problems this creates and explain how to fix each.
questions.answer: Problems: (1) No keyboard operability — div elements are not focusable and do not respond to arrow keys or Enter. Fix: add tabindex='0', role='listbox', and keyboard event handlers for arrow keys, Enter, and Escape. (2) No screen reader announcements — divs have no semantic meaning, so screen readers cannot announce the dropdown state or options. Fix: add ARIA roles (role='option'), aria-expanded to indicate open/closed state, and aria-selected for the current selection. (3) No focus management — opening the dropdown does not move focus to the options, and closing does not return focus. Fix: move focus to the first option when opened, trap focus within the dropdown while open, and return focus to the trigger when closed.

rubric.key: Identifies keyboard accessibility problem with fix
Identifies screen reader problem with ARIA fix
Identifies focus management problem with fix

questions.question: A product page has a CLS score of 0.35 (poor). The page contains a hero image without width/height attributes, a web font that loads 2 seconds after the page renders, and a cookie consent banner that pushes content down. Propose design-level fixes for each issue.
questions.answer: Fixes: (1) Hero image: specify explicit width and height attributes or use CSS aspect-ratio to reserve space before the image loads. This prevents the layout from jumping when the image finally renders. (2) Web font: use font-display: swap to show text immediately in a fallback system font, then swap to the custom font when loaded. Better yet, select a web font with similar metrics to the fallback font to minimize the visible text reflow. (3) Cookie banner: use a fixed-position banner at the bottom of the viewport instead of inserting it into the document flow. A fixed banner overlays content without pushing it, causing zero layout shift. Alternatively, reserve the banner space in the initial layout.

rubric.key: Image fix with aspect-ratio or explicit dimensions
Cookie banner fix with fixed positioning
Explains CLS impact for each fix

major_assignments.title: Responsive Web Design Project
major_assignments.description: Design a complete responsive website for a real client or organization. Deliver designs for mobile, tablet, and desktop with a focus on responsive behavior, accessibility compliance, and web performance considerations.
objectives: Design a responsive system that works across all viewport sizes
Ensure WCAG 2.1 AA compliance across all designs
Address web performance through design decisions
Test across devices and assistive technologies
deliverables: Responsive design system: grid, breakpoints, responsive components
High-fidelity designs for mobile (375px), tablet (768px), and desktop (1440px) — minimum 8 page types
Interactive prototype for the primary user flow at all three breakpoints
Accessibility compliance checklist with evidence for each criterion
Performance design audit documenting image optimization, font loading, and CLS prevention

criteria: Grid system is flexible and content-driven
Components adapt meaningfully at breakpoints
Typography scales appropriately across sizes
Content priority shifts are logical for each viewport
Strong visual hierarchy at all viewport sizes
Navigation adapts appropriately for each context
Forms are optimized for each input context (touch vs. keyboard)
Page patterns are well-executed across the site
Color contrast meets WCAG AA ratios
Heading hierarchy is semantic and logical
Focus states and keyboard navigation designed
Form labels, error messages, and ARIA patterns specified
Image optimization strategy documented
CLS prevention measures in all layouts
Font loading strategy specified
Above-the-fold content prioritized
Thoughtful responsive system with content-driven breakpoints
High-quality designs across all viewport sizes
WCAG 2.1 AA compliance documented and verified
Performance-conscious design decisions throughout

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on responsive design, web accessibility, and performance

responsive_labs.description: Weekly labs designing responsive components and testing for accessibility

web_design_project.description: Complete responsive website design with accessibility and performance optimization

accessibility_audit.description: Comprehensive accessibility audit of a live website using WCAG 2.1 criteria

final_presentation.description: Professional presentation of web design project with responsive and accessibility demonstrations

course_policies.attendance: Attendance expected for all sessions. Accessibility testing labs require screen reader practice that cannot be replicated independently. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Labs are due weekly for iterative feedback. Late work penalized 10% per day. The final project has a firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer review of designs is encouraged. All design work must be individual. Sharing Figma component libraries is permitted but must be attributed.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs must be original. Using website templates or pre-made themes is prohibited. Reference existing sites for inspiration with proper attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma, axe DevTools browser extension, screen reader (VoiceOver or NVDA), Chrome DevTools for Lighthouse, and access to multiple device sizes for testing.
